feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced during growth; switch to higher-potassium/lower-nitrogen at flowering

commonIssues: Frost-tender: mature vines may die back to the ground after exposure to the mid-20s F (around -4 C); needs protection or overwintering below freezing.
Drought-sensitive shallow roots — drought reduces growth, flowering and fruit set and increases Fusarium susceptibility; keep soil evenly moist but never saturated.
Overwatering / poor drainage promotes Fusarium wilt, Phytophthora root/crown rot and nematode damage; productive lifespan is often only 3-4 years before replanting.
Vigorous tendril climber to ~10 m — needs a strong trellis, fence or wired wall that can withstand wind.
In containers, start in roughly a 30 cm (about 28 L) pot and pot up; the 95 L+ minimum reflects a mature fruiting vine, which is best grown in the largest practical container or in-ground.
Pests: spider mites (can defoliate), caterpillars/Lepidoptera larvae, stink bugs, aphids, snails and root-knot nematodes; viral woodiness disease deforms fruit.

source.citation: UF/IFAS Extension, HS1406 'The Passion Fruit in Florida' — optimal 65-90 F, cold death in the mid-20s F, chilling injury below 59 F, USDA zone 9b+, full sun, small-but-frequent irrigation with drought/flood sensitivity, well-drained soil pH ~6-7.5, strong wind-resistant trellis, purple types self-compatible, peak production midsummer-fall, 3-4 year productive life.

UC ANR Cooperative Extension Ventura County, 'Passion Fruit' — optimal 68-82 F, purple variety frost-tender, requires a lot of water but overwatering promotes disease, frequent balanced fertilizer during the growing season, trained on strong supports like grapes on 8-12 ft centers, purple flowers self-compatible, fast-draining sandy loam pH 6.5-7, susceptible to fusarium wilt/nematodes/crown rot (replant every 3-4 years).

RHS 'How to grow passion flowers' growing guide — container culture starting in a 30 cm (12 in) pot, water thoroughly when surface is dry / weekly in dry spells and less in winter, feed every 4-6 weeks during the growing season (Mar-Oct) with no winter feeding, climbs via tendrils on a trellis/wired wall to ~10 m, edible types need a 10-16 C minimum.

Confidence note — coldHardyMinC set to -3 C as a conservative damage threshold: UF/IFAS reports mature-vine aboveground death in the mid-20s F (about -4 C) but chilling injury already below 59 F, so this single number compresses a sensitivity range. heatStressC (32 C) derived from the 90 F optimal-range ceiling combined with UC ANR's note that inland plants dislike hot dry heat. Container idealMin/MaxLiters (95-150 L) are reasoned estimates for a mature in-container vine extrapolated from the RHS 30 cm starter pot and the species' in-ground vigor; no extension source gives an exact liter figure.
